"Sec. 1
The equation $\frac{1}{3} x^{4}+5|x|=7$ involves a combination of a quartic term and an absolute value term. To find the number of real solutions, we can analyze the behavior of the function $f(x) = \frac{1}{3} x^{4} + 5|x|$.

Since $|x|$ is a piecewise function, we can split the equation into two cases: $x \geq 0$ and $x < 0$. For $x \geq 0$, $|x| = x$, so the equation becomes $\frac{1}{3} x^{4} + 5x = 7$. For $x < 0$, $|x| = -x$, so the equation becomes $\frac{1}{3} x^{4} - 5x = 7$.

Sec. 2

The equation $\frac{1}{3} x^{4} + 5x = 7$ will have at most one solution in this interval. Similarly, for $x < 0$, the function $f(x) = \frac{1}{3} x^{4} - 5x$ is also positive for $x < 0$ and increasing for $x < 0$. The equation $\frac{1}{3} x^{4} - 5x = 7$ will also have at most one solution in this interval.
